claim: AI is fundamentally a revolution in computation (not communication like internet/mobile/cloud) - it synthesizes new information and makes decisions, changing how information is processed at a foundational level
actionable: Shift your mental model: instead of thinking about AI as a communication tool, understand it as a computation engine that fundamentally changes business physics and economic models

claim: Services is becoming the new software - AI agents turn $650B software market into $10T services market because you're selling the work (agent execution) not the tool, exemplified by $400B US legal services market
actionable: If building AI products, reposition from selling tools to selling services/outcomes by deploying agents that execute on behalf of customers rather than enabling them to execute

claim: Inference-time compute (thinking/reasoning before output) is the critical inflection point - agents that can test logical paths, spot errors, and course-correct internally before responding are qualitatively different from previous models
actionable: Prioritize models with inference-time reasoning (o1, DeepSeek R1) for tasks requiring error-checking or multi-step problem solving, as token-by-token generation fails on complex reasoning

claim: Commercially-viable AGI definition: if an agent encounters an error, reads it, decides to try a different tool, and persists for hours until the job is done - that IS AGI in practice, independent of philosophical definitions
actionable: Stop debating AGI philosophy and instead measure agent capability by whether it can autonomously troubleshoot, adapt tools, and persist toward goals - build/evaluate agents by this functional standard

claim: Dark factories (fully autonomous code deployment without human review) use multi-agent swarms: coder agent writes code, independent red-team agent attacks it thousands of times/second, code only deploys when attacker mathematically exhausts breaking capability
actionable: Implement multi-agent verification loops with independent adversarial/red-team agents for high-stakes agent outputs instead of relying on single-agent code quality or human review

claim: The M.A.D. framework for founders: Moats (wrap product around durable customer needs not fast-changing tech), Affordance (create paths of least resistance - hide complexity), Diffusion (exploit gap between model capability velocity and corporate adoption speed)
actionable: Stop building features around latest model capabilities; instead build affordances (interfaces that feel obvious to non-technical users) and capitalize on enterprise's slow adoption of existing AI models

claim: Intelligence is becoming like aluminum (historically precious, now disposable) - PhD-level skills once worth 20 years of study now invoked instantly and discarded, creating economic shift equivalent to aluminum after electrolysis discovery
actionable: Revalue what humans offer: with disposable intelligence, focus on human meaning, taste, context, and relationships rather than raw cognitive capability

claim: Notion rewrite of 8 million lines of code in 6 weeks using agents required architectural dependency mapping before translation - not hallucinated spaghetti but carefully orchestrated refactoring
actionable: When using agents for large-scale code generation, implement architectural mapping and dependency analysis phases before code generation to ensure maintainability

claim: AI-designed infrastructure (antennas, chips, supply chains) will look alien and non-symmetric because algorithms optimize for physics/performance not human aesthetics, but will vastly outperform human-designed equivalents
actionable: Don't reject AI-generated designs on aesthetic grounds; validate them empirically against functional requirements since optimization constraints are often invisible to human intuition
